meta.title: NCLEX Practice — Evidence-Based Practice (NR449) Week 3
meta.subtitle: Week 3 · Research Design · Types of Research · Theory & Frameworks · 30 items
meta.footnote: NR449 Week 3 practice · 30 items · rationale after each answer

questions.stem: A nursing research committee is reviewing five proposals submitted by staff nurses. Classify each proposal by the design approach it uses.

rows.t: Comparing the mean blood pressures of clients who completed cardiac rehabilitation after a myocardial infarction with those of clients who did not
Investigating what it means to be a nursing student who also works 20 or more hours a week
Collecting post-rehabilitation blood pressure readings and also interviewing those same clients about how the program shaped their recovery
Living within a rural community for two years to learn how its members view illness and healing
Counting how many days elapse before clients refill a newly prescribed antihypertensive and analyzing the pattern by age group

questions.rat: This module defines a quantitative design as one that measures data and analyzes numbers (blood pressures, refill intervals), a qualitative design as one that seeks the lived experiences of participants or the meaning of a phenomenon (what it means to work while in nursing school; how a community views illness), and a mixed-method design as one in which both qualitative and quantitative data are available. Adding the clients' lived experiences to the blood pressure study is the module's own example of what converts a quantitative study into a mixed-method one.
questions.strat: Ask what is actually being analyzed — numbers, meaning, or both.

questions.stem:
A nurse researcher wants to determine whether a structured 48-hour discharge phone call lowers the 30-day readmission rate on a heart failure unit.

Because the aim is to test a relationship between variables rather than to describe a phenomenon, the study is [1]. Because the data to be analyzed are readmission counts, the design approach is [2]. Before any specific design can be chosen, the first of the three phases of decision making requires the researcher to [3].

blanks.label: [1] Aim of the study
opts: exploratory
confirmatory
emergent

blanks.label: [2] Design approach
opts: qualitative
quantitative
mixed-method

blanks.label: [3] First phase of decision making
opts: identify the assumptions about the knowledge the study will produce
develop detailed plans for how the study will be implemented
select the design that fits the purpose of the study

questions.rat: The module states that confirmatory studies are based on testing a relationship between variables, while exploratory studies are usually qualitative or mixed-method and describe a phenomenon. Readmission counts are measured numbers, which is this module's definition of a quantitative design. The three phases run in a fixed order — identify assumptions, select a design, implementation — and it is the assumptions phase that tells the researcher whether a quantitative, qualitative, or mixed-method design is required, so it cannot be skipped ahead of choosing the design or planning its execution.
questions.strat: Testing a relationship = confirmatory; assumptions come before the design, and the design before the plan.

questions.stem: A faculty mentor asks a student to name the descriptive design used in each of six study summaries. Match each summary to its design.

rows.t: Teen clients are asked, at the moment of their cancer diagnosis, what their top information need is
Clients aged 5 to 50 years are asked, at the moment of their cancer diagnosis, what their top information need is
Teen cancer clients are asked about their top information need at diagnosis, at first treatment, and again at six months
Detailed information is gathered by varied techniques from one campus's graduating nursing class about their priority needs after graduation
A small group's adherence to blood pressure medication is reviewed repeatedly over time to follow each person's own response
Nursing students who work 20 or more hours a week are interviewed to uncover the meaning of that experience

questions.rat: Survey and cross-sectional designs both use a single point in time; this module separates them by whether the sample differs on a key characteristic — teens only is a single sample characteristic (survey), while ages 5 to 50 differ on age (cross-sectional). Longitudinal data are collected at select points over time to describe change, a case study collects detailed information by varied techniques about a single individual, program, event, or action, single subject design studies interventions against individuals' own responses at baseline or ongoing, and phenomenology seeks the meaning of a lived experience among those who lived it.
questions.strat: Single point in time? Then ask whether the sample varies on a key characteristic — that is what splits survey from cross-sectional.

questions.stem: A nurse researcher wants to understand how the members of a remote community understand their relationship to the environment and to healing. The researcher plans to live within the community and take part in its daily life for the next two years. Which design is the researcher describing?
opts: Phenomenology, because the study centers on the meaning of a lived experience shared by participants
Ethnography, because the researcher immerses in the culture over an extended period of time
Grounded theory, because a theory of process is developed from analysis of participants' words
Case study, because varied techniques collect detailed information about a single group
questions.rat: This module defines ethnography as a cultural study in which the researcher immerses themselves in the culture over a period of time, and its example is a researcher living within a tribe for years to understand their view of the environment — the immersion plus duration is the tell. Phenomenology would fit only if the aim were the meaning of one specific lived experience among people who had experienced it, grounded theory builds a theory of process, action, or interaction from participants' words, and a case study explores a single individual, program, event, or action rather than a whole culture.
questions.strat: Immersion plus time equals ethnography; meaning of one experience equals phenomenology.

questions.stem:
A researcher is studying whether a 30-minute screen-free evening routine increases the number of hours adolescents sleep. Each participant's age is recorded to describe the sample. Partway through data collection the researcher learns that several participants share a bedroom with a sibling who studies late with the light on.

Classify each element of this study.

blanks.label: [1] The screen-free evening routine
opts: independent variable
dependent variable
extraneous variable

blanks.label: [2] Hours of sleep
opts: independent variable
dependent variable
descriptive variable

blanks.label: [3] The sibling's late-night light
opts: independent variable
extraneous variable
dependent variable

blanks.label: [4] The recorded ages
opts: research variables introduced to measure an expected effect
descriptive variables not influenced by the study or researcher
extraneous variables that confuse interpretation of the outcome

questions.rat: This module defines the independent variable as the cause in cause and effect, added to the study to measure an effect, and the dependent variable as the effect or outcome — so the routine is independent and hours of sleep dependent. An extraneous variable is not part of the planned research but influences the outcome and may confuse interpretation; the module's own parallel example is blue-light exposure occurring outside the research framework. Age is a descriptive variable: a measurable participant characteristic that is not influenced by the study or the researcher.
questions.strat: Cause = independent, effect = dependent, uninvited influence = extraneous, description of the sample = descriptive.

questions.stem: A student proposes a phenomenological study of what caregiving means to the spouses of clients living with dementia, then asks the faculty mentor for help naming the independent and dependent variables. Which response by the mentor reflects this module's teaching?
opts: The caregiving role would be the independent variable and the reported burden the dependent variable
Variables are the basis of quantitative research and are not used in a qualitative design like this
Both variables can be named once the interview data have been analyzed into themes
Spouse age and years of caregiving would serve as the extraneous variables here
questions.rat: The Variables section of this module states directly that variables are not used in qualitative research but are the basis of quantitative research, so a phenomenological study — which seeks the meaning of a lived experience — has no independent or dependent variable to name. Assigning cause-and-effect roles to the caregiving experience treats a qualitative aim as a confirmatory quantitative one, and extraneous variables belong to that same quantitative framework. (Note the module's evaluation section separately asks whether design variables such as age are identified when appraising a qualitative article; the Variables section is its direct statement on this question.)
questions.strat: If the design is chasing meaning rather than measurement, stop hunting for variables.

questions.stem: A nurse researcher proposes a three-year study of a new fall-prevention protocol on a medical-surgical unit. The unit's census falls sharply every summer, the protocol calls for one group of clients to go without the intervention, the researcher has never carried a study past six months, and the grant covers only the first year. Which of these are design-selection factors this module says must be weighed before the design is finalized? Select all that apply.
opts: Whether the client population will still be available on the unit throughout the data-collection window
Whether going without the protocol places that group of clients in danger
Whether the researcher's own strengths and weaknesses could become limitations of the study
Whether the funding and materials on hand can carry the project through its full time frame
Whether the sample will be large enough for the findings to reach statistical significance
Whether the unit's policy manual will be revised once the findings are reported
questions.rat: This module lists the factors that influence design selection: variables (protecting participants is an ethical role of the nurse researcher, and putting participants in danger is a reason not to implement a design), sampling and measurements (is the population available during the research time frame?), the researcher's strengths and weaknesses, the time frame, the amount of control required, the audience, and resources such as money, materials, and participant pools. The size needed for statistical significance and the policy change that may follow are products of the study, not factors that determine which design can be used.
questions.strat: Design-selection factors are all about what the researcher can feasibly and ethically do; anything about the results comes later.

questions.stem: A journal club is sorting five studies by the relationship design each one used. Match each study to its design.

rows.t: Examining the relationship between maternal age and anxiety among mothers scheduled for a planned cesarean birth
Comparing the level of a single variable, treatment adherence, across two separate populations
Determining whether a participant's age forecasts that participant's medication adherence
Analyzing teens' own words to build an explanation of how they come to take their medication consistently
Applying a preconceived model to test whether adding a support group affects the coping skills of teens with anxiety

questions.rat: This module defines a correlation as a relationship between two variables in a single population or one variable in two populations, so both of the first two studies are correlations. A predictive study relationship runs between a predictor variable and an outcome variable, which is the age-to-adherence study. Grounded theory develops a theory of process, action, or interaction based on analysis of participants' words. A test of model fit uses a preconceived model to test theories of causal relationships between variables.
questions.strat: Correlation describes a relationship, prediction aims one variable at an outcome, grounded theory builds from words, model fit starts from the model.

questions.stem: A study reports that clients who received a new mobility protocol walked farther at discharge than clients who did not. Every client received the protocol before walking distance was measured, and the statistical effect of the protocol was strong. During the same months, however, the unit added a full-time physical therapist and changed its staffing ratio. Which criterion for establishing causality has this study failed to meet?
opts: Temporality, because the sequence between the independent and dependent variables is unclear
Influence, because the effect of the independent variable is not statistically sound
Specificity, because competing explanations for the relationship were not controlled
Reliability, because the walking-distance measurement was not repeated over time
questions.rat: This module names three criteria that must be considered in a causality study: temporality (the time sequence between the independent and dependent variables supports causation), influence (the statistical soundness of the effect the independent variable has on the dependent variable), and specificity (the elimination or control of other explanations for the relationship). The sequence here is intact and the effect is strong, but the new therapist and the staffing change are uncontrolled alternative explanations, which is exactly what specificity requires the researcher to rule out. Reliability of measurement is not one of the three criteria this module lists.
questions.strat: Temporality = order, influence = strength, specificity = nothing else explains it.

questions.stem: A nurse researcher introduces a hand-off communication bundle on one telemetry unit and measures error reports before the bundle and again after it. No comparison unit is used, and the nurses on the unit were not assigned to groups in any way. Which design has the researcher used?
opts: Experimental design, because an intervention was introduced and the resulting outcome was measured
Quasi-experimental design, because a treatment was given without randomization or a control group
Causal-comparative design, because groups are matched on the independent variables of interest
Time series analysis, because a baseline was measured before the treatment was implemented
questions.rat: This module defines quasi-experimental design as introducing a treatment to a group with no random assignment and no control group, which is precisely this study. An experimental design would require randomly assigning subjects to a treatment group and a control group and comparing the outcomes. A causal-comparative/case-control design matches already-formed groups by independent variables and analyzes dependent variables for differences. Time series analysis measures a baseline, implements the treatment, then collects outcome data over time at specified dates or periods rather than at one point afterward.
questions.strat: No randomization and no control group drops experimental down to quasi-experimental.

questions.stem: A nurse appraises a published qualitative study of nursing students who work while enrolled. The article names the design in standard research language, gives the rationale for choosing it, ties that design to the research question, and identifies the variables that could influence outcomes. It reports the resulting themes but never describes how participants were recruited and interviewed or how the researchers' choices were made as the design took shape. Using this module's criteria, which conclusion is best supported?
opts: The design that was chosen cannot be linked to the research question as the article is written
The rationale for the design choice was not provided anywhere in the published article
The study could not be replicated, and the emerging design decisions are not explained
The variables that could influence the students' outcomes were left undefined by the researchers
questions.rat: This module's evaluation criteria ask whether the study can be replicated by others and, in qualitative design, whether the researcher has explained the decisions made as the design emerged. The article satisfies the other criteria the module lists — the design is identified in standard language, the rationale is given, the design is linked to the research question, and the influencing variables are identified — so the only unmet criterion is the missing procedural detail that would let another researcher reproduce the work and follow how the design developed.
questions.strat: Walk the evaluation checklist item by item; the criterion nothing in the article addresses is the answer.

questions.stem:
A nurse researcher surveys 400 adults in one rural county to determine how many are living with untreated hypertension and what they report about access to care. No comparison group is used and no intervention is applied.
Which conclusion is supportable by this design?
opts: The prevalence of untreated hypertension in this county is 34%, indicating a need for screening services.
Living in a rural county causes adults with hypertension to leave the condition untreated over time.
Adults who enroll in the new clinic will end up with lower untreated hypertension rates than those who do not.
The county's screening program lowered the rate of untreated hypertension across the study period.
questions.rat: Descriptive research answers what, when, and how questions and is invaluable for documenting the prevalence, nature, and intensity of a condition and the need for new or additional services. It explicitly does not answer why a phenomenon occurs or establish cause and effect, which rules out the causal claim, the predictive claim about future clinic enrollees, and the claim that a program lowered rates.
questions.strat: Descriptive tells you how much and how many, never why - it is hypothesis-generating, not hypothesis-testing.

questions.stem:
A student is critiquing a descriptive survey study of vaccine hesitancy in which participants were questioned in their own homes.
Which statements about descriptive research are accurate? Select all that apply.
opts: The design cannot be used to establish a cause-and-effect relationship between the variables studied.
Respondents may answer in a socially desirable way rather than truthfully.
The choice and wording of the questionnaire items can influence what the findings show.
Findings may not be generalizable, depending on the type and size of the sample obtained.
Data must be gathered in a controlled setting rather than where the participants live.
Subjects must be assigned at random to comparison groups before any data are collected.
questions.rat: The guide lists inability to establish causation, untruthful or socially desirable responses, question wording effects, and limited generalizability as the limitations of descriptive research. Collecting data in the respondent's natural environment (home, childcare, or educational setting) is listed as a strength, not a requirement to avoid, and random assignment belongs to experimental designs, not descriptive ones.
questions.strat: Sort each option into strength, limitation, or wrong design family before marking it.

questions.stem: Match each proposed study to the type of descriptive study it represents.

rows.t: A team watches and records staff hand-hygiene behavior on a unit; the staff themselves provide no input
A questionnaire is e-mailed to 12,000 licensed nurses in one state to collect feedback on staffing
A one-day chart audit estimates how many residents of a nursing home currently have a pressure injury
One cohort of new graduates is measured for burnout every year for the next ten years
Investigators examine whether nurses' sleep hours and medication-error counts move in opposite directions, controlling neither
One client with a rare metabolic disorder is studied in depth to generate a hypothesis for later work

questions.rat: Observational studies collect data from the researcher's view of behavior with no input from respondents; surveys collect respondent-reported feedback and suit very large samples; cross-sectional studies are a snapshot of frequency at one point in time; longitudinal studies gather data repeatedly over an extended period; correlation measures the relationship between two or more variables the researcher does not control; case studies are in-depth study of an individual or group that widen the scope for later work.
questions.strat: Ask who supplies the data and over how much time - that pair separates observational, survey, cross-sectional, and longitudinal.

questions.stem:
A descriptive study reports that as the number of continuing-education hours completed by unit nurses rises, the unit's catheter-associated urinary tract infection rate falls. The researchers controlled neither variable.
How should the nurse interpret this finding?
opts: A negative correlation exists, so the data support prediction but not a causal conclusion.
A positive correlation exists, so the data support prediction but not a causal conclusion.
A negative correlation exists, so the data establish that added education lowers the infection rate.
A zero correlation exists, so the data support neither prediction nor a causal conclusion.
questions.rat: Variables moving in opposite directions define a negative correlation; both moving the same direction would be positive, and no relationship would be zero. Correlation studies can be used to make predictions about relationships, but the researcher has no control over the variables, so correlation does not equal causation.
questions.strat: Name the direction first, then refuse the causal sentence - two decisions, not one.

questions.stem:
A nurse is appraising a proposed study of how families experience a relative's admission to hospice.
Indicate whether each feature is consistent with qualitative research.

rows.t: Uses inductive reasoning to move from the participants' accounts toward understanding
Studies the phenomenon in the naturalistic setting where the people involved live daily
Tests whether an intervention produced a measurable change in the families
May supply the information needed to pursue a later quantitative study
Relies on a representative sample so results generalize to larger groups with confidence
Interprets the phenomenon in terms of the meanings the people involved bring to it

questions.rat: Qualitative research is inductive, studies phenomena in naturalistic settings, interprets meanings people bring to an experience, and may provide information necessary to pursue quantitative studies. It explicitly does not test interventions, and generalizing from a representative sample is a characteristic of quantitative designs, which apply interventions to samples in order to generalize to broader populations.
questions.strat: Any row about testing an intervention or generalizing to a population has crossed over into quantitative.

questions.stem:
A team interviews 18 family caregivers, codes the transcripts interpretively, and reports themes. The published paper names the approach content analysis. A student asks how content analysis should be classified.
Which response is correct?
opts: A data analysis method rather than a distinct design, used when a study fits no formal classification.
A distinct qualitative design, defined by the use of interview transcripts as its primary source of data.
A quantitative design, because interpretive codes are converted into counts and analyzed statistically.
A variant of grounded theory, because interpretive coding of transcripts produces themes and patterns.
questions.rat: The guide states content analysis is technically not a specific qualitative research design but is more accurately a data analysis method for interpreting meaning in verbal responses or written documents, often used when a qualitative study does not fit neatly into one of the more formal classifications. Grounded theory is a design whose aim is to develop a theory from the data, which content analysis does not claim to do.
questions.strat: If it describes how the data were interpreted rather than how the study was structured, it is a method, not a design.

questions.stem:
A nurse researcher plans an ethnographic study of the culture of a hospital's night-shift staff and intends to work alongside them for several months.
Which statement describes the researcher's role in this design?
opts: Becoming an active participant in the group in order to learn from its members without changing the culture.
Remaining outside the group entirely so that the members' usual behavior is not altered by the researcher's presence.
Assigning staff to different shift patterns so that cultural differences between the groups can be compared.
Interviewing members individually until no new themes emerge, without entering the work setting itself.
questions.rat: In ethnography the researcher's aim is to learn from, rather than study, the members of a culture without changing it, and participant observation is very common - the researcher becomes an active participant to understand the group's experiences and worldview. Staying outside the group describes detached observation, assigning staff to conditions is manipulation belonging to experimental work, and interview-only data collection describes a different qualitative approach.
questions.strat: Ethnography = culture + participant observation; the researcher joins in rather than watching from the hallway.

questions.stem:
A nurse manager reads a qualitative study describing how clients experience their first month on home dialysis and wants to apply it on the unit.
Which use of this evidence fits what the study can offer?
opts: Preparing anticipatory guidance about the range of responses clients may have that month.
Setting a target for the percentage of clients who will adapt within one month of starting dialysis.
Deciding whether home dialysis produces better adaptation than in-center dialysis does.
Establishing how often distress occurs among home dialysis clients in the first month.
questions.rat: Nurses use qualitative evidence to understand clients' experiences and the range of their responses, to provide anticipatory guidance and coaching, and to improve nurse-client communication. Setting a numeric target, comparing the effectiveness of two modalities, and establishing how often a problem occurs all require measurement and comparison, which are purposes of quantitative or descriptive-prevalence work.
questions.strat: Qualitative evidence tells you what it is like, so it feeds teaching and communication, not numbers or effect claims.

questions.stem:
A nurse is contrasting the two broad research traditions for a journal club.
Qualitative research is [1] in nature and uses [2] to explain a phenomenon. Quantitative research uses [3] and develops knowledge through [4].

blanks.label: [1] Qualitative reasoning
opts: deductive
inductive
experimental

blanks.label: [2] Qualitative data
opts: words rather than numbers
numbers rather than words
statistics rather than themes

blanks.label: [3] Quantitative reasoning
opts: inductive reasoning
deductive reasoning
interpretive coding

blanks.label: [4] Quantitative knowledge development
opts: a hypothesis-generating description
a hypothesis-testing process
participant observation

questions.rat: Qualitative research is explanatory, descriptive, and inductive in nature and uses words rather than numbers to explain a phenomenon. Quantitative research uses deductive reasoning and develops knowledge by a hypothesis-testing process: state them, test them, disprove them, or confirm findings by retesting. Hypothesis-generating is the phrase the guide attaches to descriptive research, not to quantitative research.
questions.strat: Qualitative goes from stories up to ideas (inductive); quantitative goes from a hypothesis down to data (deductive).

questions.stem:
A staff nurse notes that experimental designs are called the gold standard for evidence-based practice yet asks why so few nursing studies are randomized controlled trials.
Which response is accurate?
opts: The high control an experiment requires is hard to achieve in practice, and withholding treatment can be unethical.
Nursing questions usually concern the meaning of lived experience, so a design that tests effects rarely fits them well.
Random assignment demands very large samples, which most nursing units cannot recruit or afford to study.
Findings from experiments cannot be generalized beyond the single site where the intervention was tested.
questions.rat: The guide attributes the rarity of experiments in nursing and healthcare to the difficulty of achieving the high levels of control that characterize experiments in an applied setting, and to the fact that manipulating an independent variable or withholding treatment is not always desirable or ethical. Nursing does ask effect questions, sample size is not the stated barrier, and generalizability is a strength of experimental work because of random assignment.
questions.strat: Experiments are rare in practice for two reasons the guide names: control is hard, and withholding care may be unethical.

questions.stem:
Investigators interview nurses who sustained a needlestick injury during the past five years and ask each one to recall whether a safety-engineered device was in use at the time. Both the device use and the injury had already occurred when the data were collected, and no uninjured comparison group was assembled.
Which statement identifies this design and its central weakness?
opts: Ex post facto research; temporality cannot be proven because the investigator depends on the subject's recall.
Causal-comparative research; the groups were formed from an inherent characteristic the subjects already possessed.
Case-control research; each affected subject was matched with an unaffected subject on key characteristics.
Time series research; the outcome was measured repeatedly rather than compared against a separate group.
questions.rat: Ex post facto research observes relationships between naturally occurring differences after the fact, when both the independent and dependent variables have already occurred; this weakens cause-and-effect conclusions because the researcher cannot prove temporality and depends on the subject's recall to establish that the independent variable came first. Causal-comparative groups subjects by an inherent characteristic such as gender or region, case-control matches subjects who have a characteristic with subjects who do not, and time series measures one group repeatedly around an intervention.
questions.strat: Data collected after both variables have happened, resting on recall = ex post facto and no proof of temporality.

questions.stem:
A medical unit measures its fall rate per 1,000 patient-days monthly for three months, then implements a new mobility protocol and measures the rate monthly for the next twelve months. No other unit is used for comparison.
How should the nurse classify this study?
opts: Quasi-experimental; the subjects serve as their own comparison group against the preintervention baseline.
Experimental; the repeated measurement over twelve months substitutes for random assignment of the subjects.
Longitudinal descriptive; repeated measures over time describe a trend but no intervention was applied here.
Case-control; the months carrying the protocol are matched against the months without the protocol.
questions.rat: This is a time series design, a type of quasi-experimental design with only one group: a baseline is measured, the intervention is applied, and measures are taken periodically afterward, with subjects serving as their own comparison group. Repeated measurement never substitutes for random assignment, an intervention clearly was applied so this is not purely descriptive, and case-control matches subjects with and without a characteristic rather than time periods.
questions.strat: One group, a baseline, then repeated measures after an intervention = time series, which is quasi-experimental.

questions.stem:
A unit EBP council is appraising a published study of a central-line dressing-change bundle before recommending it. The report states the problem, describes the sample and methods, and reports the outcomes, but no theory is named or described anywhere in the article. A council member asks what the study loses by leaving out a theoretical framework.
Which response by the nurse leading the appraisal is accurate?
opts: The reported p values cannot be trusted because no hypothesis was tested.
The results carry less credibility and are harder for others to replicate.
The study cannot be classified as either quantitative or qualitative research.
The literature review cannot be used to justify the variables the authors measured.
questions.rat: The module teaches that the theory is the conceptual foundation, or backbone, of a study: a strong theoretical framework brings credibility to the results and allows the study to be replicated. A missing framework does not invalidate the statistics, erase the study's design classification, or void the literature review; it weakens the conceptual justification and reproducibility of the work.
questions.strat: Framework = backbone. Take the backbone out and the study still stands up, just weakly: less credibility, harder to replicate.

questions.stem: A graduate student is annotating a published study to show the ways its theoretical framework strengthens it. Match each observation she wrote in the margin to the way the framework strengthens the study.
shared: Specifies which variables influence the phenomenon and how they differ by circumstance
Lets the reader weigh the stated assumptions before accepting the conclusions
Ties the hypothesis to the methods and situates the study in existing knowledge
Moves the researcher past describing the phenomenon to generalizing aspects of it

rows.t: Evaluation
Connection
Generalization
Limitations

questions.rat: Per the module's interactive: Evaluation - a distinct statement of theoretical assumptions lets the reader critically evaluate the research; Connection - the framework connects the reader to existing knowledge and connects the hypothesis to the methods; Generalization - articulating assumptions lets the researcher progress from describing a phenomenon to generalizing aspects of it; Limitations - the framework identifies limits to generalization by specifying which variables influence the phenomenon and under what circumstances they differ.
questions.strat: Limitations and Generalization are the pair students swap: Generalization is the reach the theory gives you, Limitations is the fence around that reach.

questions.stem:
A student is designing a study of whether a structured pre-shift huddle reduces medication administration errors on a medical unit. She has stated the research problem, reviewed how other researchers addressed similar problems, and identified the huddle as the independent variable and error rate as the dependent variable.
According to the module's process, what is her next step in arriving at a theoretical framework?
opts: Review several relevant theories and choose the one that best explains how her variables relate.
Select the theory cited most often in the literature she reviewed and then fit her variables into it.
Adopt the nursing theory her hospital has chosen as its professional practice model.
Run the study first and then choose the theory whose concepts her results most resemble.
questions.rat: The module's sequence ends with reviewing several relevant theories, choosing the one that best explains the relationships between the key variables, and then confirming that theory's relevance to the research problem. Letting citation counts, an employer's practice model, or the data pick the theory reverses the logic: the problem and its variables drive theory selection, not the other way around.
questions.strat: Problem, then variables, then theory. If the theory is chosen before the variables are known, or after the data are in, the order is wrong.

questions.stem:
A nurse reviews a colleague's research proposal. The colleague selected a randomized design and wrote the data-collection tool first, then went back to the literature to attach a theory so the proposal 'would look stronger to the review board.'
Which critique of this proposal is most accurate?
opts: The tool should be selected first so the theory can be matched to the data actually collected.
The theory should have focused the variables and framed the design, not been added afterward.
Theory frames qualitative work; a randomized design is directed by the hypothesis alone.
Attaching the theory later is acceptable as long as the literature review supports the variables.
questions.rat: The module describes theory as influencing design: the researcher picks a topic, hypothesizes how the concepts interact, reviews the literature, and then uses a theory to focus on the specific variables of interest, forming a road map for the design. A theory bolted on after the design cannot have guided it. The module also states that theoretical frameworks may be used in both quantitative and qualitative research, so a randomized design does not exempt the proposal.
questions.strat: Road map before the trip. If the theory arrives after the design or the tool, it framed nothing.

questions.stem:
A nurse researcher is listing candidate frameworks for a study and must note, for each one, whether it is a nursing theory or a theory borrowed from another discipline.
Classify each framework using the module's examples.

rows.t: Benner's Novice-to-Expert Framework
Becker's Health Belief Model
Beck's Theory of Postpartum Depression
Bandura's Social Cognitive Theory
Roy's Adaptation Model
Prochaska's Transtheoretical Change Theory

questions.rat: The module lists Benner's Novice-to-Expert Framework, Roy's Adaptation Model, Beck's Theory of Postpartum Depression, Kolcaba's Comfort Theory, and Pender's Health Promotion Model as nursing theories, and Bandura's Social Cognitive Theory, Prochaska's Transtheoretical Change Theory, Becker's Health Belief Model, and Ajzen's Theory of Planned Behavior as theories from other disciplines that support nursing research. Beck (nursing, postpartum depression) and Becker (health belief model, outside nursing) are the pair most often confused, as are Pender's Health Promotion Model (nursing) and Becker's Health Belief Model.
questions.strat: Nurse researchers use both kinds. Sort by who wrote it, not by how clinical it sounds: Beck is nursing, Becker is not.

questions.stem:
A student is asked to name the theoretical framework of two assigned articles. One article names Roy's Adaptation Model in its title and returns to it when discussing the findings. The second never labels a framework at all; it builds on what previous studies established about the problem and reports numeric outcomes.
Complete the student's summary using the module's guidance.

The second article is typical, because [1] researchers are less likely to identify the theoretical framework. In contrast, [2] research reflects on a specific theory and always uses a theoretical framework. When no framework is labeled outright, the reader should look for it [3].

blanks.label: [1] Less likely to name the framework
opts: quantitative
qualitative
mixed-methods

blanks.label: [2] Always uses a framework
opts: quantitative
qualitative
descriptive

blanks.label: [3] Where to look
opts: in the introduction, the title, and the discussion of the findings
only in a separately headed theoretical framework section
in the statistical results, where the theory's concepts are tested
in the funding and conflict-of-interest disclosures

questions.rat: The module states that quantitative researchers are less likely to identify the theoretical framework but often use what is already known about a problem from previous research, while qualitative research reflects on a specific theory and always uses a theoretical framework. It also lists where a framework may surface: the introduction, a separate section, the title, how the concepts relate to the outcome studied, how the variables are viewed, and how the findings are considered in relation to the theory. A separate headed section is one possible location, not the only one.
questions.strat: Quantitative may hide it; qualitative always has one. Hunt the title, the introduction, and the discussion before concluding there is no framework.

questions.stem:
A shared-governance committee is critiquing a published study that used Pender's Health Promotion Model to frame a nurse-led walking program for adults with hypertension.
Which features of the report should the committee identify as weaknesses in the study's use of theory? Select all that apply.
opts: The theory is named in the introduction and is not referred to again once the results are reported.
The authors note that their findings conflict with an earlier study that used the same model but offer no theory-based explanation.
The variables are named, but the report does not describe how each was measured as an outcome.
The authors devote a separate headed section to describing the model and its major concepts.
The literature review presents prior work supporting the choice of the study's variables.
The discussion compares the study's outcomes with what the model predicts about perceived self-efficacy.
questions.rat: The module's critique questions ask whether the framework is used as a basis for comparison once data are analyzed, whether the findings are discussed in relation to the theory, whether discrepancies with other researchers using the same framework can be explained through the theory, and whether the researcher clearly describes the variables and how they relate to a measurable outcome. Options 0, 1, and 2 each fail one of those. A separate theory section, a literature review that supports the variables, and a discussion tied to the model's concepts are exactly what the critique questions ask to see.
questions.strat: A framework has to work through the whole paper. If the theory appears in the introduction and never in the discussion, it decorated the study rather than framing it.
